institution: Sector-wide typology - Indian retail digital payments
category: Social engineering - OTP phishing and vishing
risk_signals: Caller or message impersonates the bank and asks the customer to share an OTP, CVV, or PIN
Urgent pretext: account/card 'blocked', KYC expiry, reward, or refund
Spoofed caller ID or a fake website mimicking the bank
analyst_reasoning: Banks never ask for OTP/CVV/PIN, so any request to share them is fraudulent by definition. The attacker manufactures urgency to bypass the victim's caution and harvest the exact secret needed to authorise a transaction.
investigation_steps: Establish the contact channel (call/SMS/fake site) and whether caller ID was spoofed
Match the time the OTP was shared to the fraudulent transaction
Identify the phishing domain or number and whether it recurs across complaints
decision: Block the transaction if still in flight, reset credentials, and reinforce that the bank never requests OTP/CVV/PIN.
outcome: Sharing the OTP completes the fraudster's transaction; refusing to share it stops the attack outright.
lessons_learned: No legitimate bank ever asks for OTP, CVV, or PIN - that request alone identifies fraud
Manufactured urgency is the core lever of vishing
The OTP step is the last line of defence and must never be shared
